text.fullText: Little Traverse Bay Bands of Odawa Indians Natural Resource Department 7500 Odawa Circle Harbor Springs, MI 49740 Phone: (231)242-1670 Fax: (231)242-1690 February 17th, 2026 Lauren Kasparek Oceans, Wetlands, and Communities Division Office of Water (4504-T) Environmental Protection Agency 1200 Pennsylvania Avenue NW Washington, DC 20460 Re: LTBB comments on Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations (EPA-HQ-OW- 2025-2929) Dear Ms. Lauren Kasparek, On behalf of the Little Traverse Bay Bands of Odawa Indians (LTBB), please accept these comments regarding the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) proposed revisions to Clean Water Act Section 401 Water Quality Certification regulations. LTBB appreciates the opportunity to provide feedback on this important revision. LTBB’s traditional way of life and rights to hunt, fish, and gather in the 1836 Ceded Territory were reserved in the 1836 Treaty of Washington and LTBB are also signatory to the 1855 Treaty of Detroit. Article VI, Clause 2 of the United States Constitution promulgates treaties as the “…supreme Law of the Land.”1 Although the United States failed and continues to fail to uphold some of the agreed upon articles of these treaties,2 the retained rights to hunt, fish, and gather endure and this was reaffirmed by Federal Court in the case of United States v. Michigan (WD MI Case 2:73 CV 26). LTBB is party to the 2023 Great Lakes and 2007 Inland Consent Decrees entered in that case. The treaty rights to hunt, fish, and gather under the framework of sovereignty also reflect LTBB’s retention of a traditional relationship with the environment. Environmental conditions affect LTBB’s ability to exercise treaty rights as well as our relation to the environment and general wellbeing. Only the United States Congress has the authority to abrogate treaty rights.3 1 Constitution of the United States of America (1787). 2 See Blackbird, “History of the Ottawa and Chippewa Indians of Michigan” Ch. VI at para. 12 and Ch. XII at para. 3 (1887). Blackbird enlightens readers on the unapproved and unscrupulous changes by the United States to the Treaties of 1836 and 1855 after agreement and signature by Tribal Nations. See Chief Judge Fox, United States v. State of Mich., 471 F. Supp. 192 (W.D. Mich. 1979) at pg. 229. “Quite simply, he (Schoolcraft) relied upon fraud and duress…The negotiation of this treaty (1836) is rent through with deception, manipulation and double dealing.” See Judge Paul Maloney, Little Traverse Bay Bands of Odawa Indians v. Whitmer, 398 F. Supp. 3d 201 (W.D. Mich. 2019) at para. 212. “…the (1855) Treaty was not well-implemented…Indian Agents responsible…repeatedly transmitted land descriptions rife with incorrect descriptions…There were additional failures by the government… (to deliver) a certificate of the land issued to the selecting Indian...The United States also failed to make some of the promised annuity payments.” 3 See U.S. Supreme Court, La Abra Silver Mining Co. v. United States, 175 U.S. 423 (1899) at pg. 175. “It has been adjudged that Congress by legislation, and so far as the people and authorities of the United States are concerned, Scope of Certification Section 401 was designed to ensure that federally permitted projects protect water quality as whole. In fact, the Supreme Court confirmed through their ruling in PUD No.1 of Jefferson County v. Washington Dept. of Ecology that Section 401 applies to the “activity as a whole 4.” By limiting Section 401 review to “discharge”, the proposed rule would ignore other impacts to water quality such as flow, temperature, habitat destruction/fragmentation, and cumulative impacts that may affect treaty reserved rights and designated uses of a waterbody. LTBB requests that the EPA maintains the broader, activity-based scope of review as established by the Supreme Court’s ruling in PUD No.1 of Jefferson County v. Washington Dept. of Ecology and reaffirmed in the 2023 Rule. Request for Certification (list of components) The proposed rule’s limitation to a defined list of components would create a rigid framework that fails to acknowledge a certifying authority’s own water quality standards and would lead to uninformed decisions. The language in the proposed rule to include “readily available water quality-related materials on any potential discharges,” is vague and seems to focus on point- source discharges. However, as pointed out in the prior section on the “scope of certification,” projects often have impacts that extend far beyond their discharges. LTBB is concerned that limiting the components required for analysis will lead to an inability to assess all the proposed project’s impacts to tribal resources and consequently, our treaty-reserved rights. LTBB requests that the EPA maintains the current approach, where certifying agencies may require additional components of applicants. Reasonable Period of Time The proposed provisions to eliminate extensions for public notice procedures and force majeure events would create rigid timelines that would lead to incomplete analysis and premature decisions on water quality certifications. Projects often change substantially over the course of an application window, and force majeure events, such as natural disasters, create entirely new conditions that may significantly affect the decision-making process. Additionally, tribal governments often have internal procedures between tribal departments, tribal councils, Tribal Historic Preservation Officers, and the tribal community that can extend the process beyond a year. By limiting the reasonable period of time, the proposed rule would infringe on tribal processes and sovereignty. LTBB requests that the EPA maintains the 2023 Rule’s approach to the reasonable period of time, out of respect to tribal sovereignty. could abrogate a treaty made between this country and another which had been negotiated by the President and approved by the Senate.” 4 See U.S. Supreme Court, PUD No.1 of Jefferson County v. Washington Dept. of Ecology, (92-1911), 511 U.S. 700 (1994). If §401 consisted solely of subsection (a), which refers to a state certification that a "discharge" will comply with certain provisions of the Act, petitioners' assessment of the scope of the State's certification authority would have considerable force. Section 401, however, also contains subsection (d), which expands the State's authority to impose conditions on the certification of a project. Section 401(d) provides that any certification shall set forth "any effluent limitations and other limitations . . . necessary to assure that any applicant" will comply with various provisions of the Act and appropriate state law requirements. 33 U.S.C. § 1341(d) (emphasis added). The language of this subsection contradicts petitioners' claim that the State may only impose water quality limitations specifically tied to a "discharge." The text refers to the compliance of the applicant, not the discharge. Section 401(d) thus allows the State to impose "other limitations" on the project in general to assure compliance with various provisions of the Clean Water Act and with "any other appropriate requirement of State law." Although the dissent asserts that this interpretation of §401(d) renders §401(a)(1) superfluous, infra, at 4, we see no such anomaly. Section 401(a)(1) identifies the category of activities subject to certification namely those with discharges. And §401(d) is most reasonably read as authorizing additional conditions and limitations on the activity as a whole once the threshold condition, the existence of a discharge, is satisfied Modifications The ability to modify certifications is a critical tool that upholds tribal sovereignty and allows certifying agencies to adapt to changing conditions (water quality standards, updated science, changing environmental conditions, additions/removals to T&E species lists, etc). These are particularly important in long-term projects, such as FERC-licensed hydropower dams where licenses can last from 30-50 years. As mentioned in our introduction, only Congress has the power to abrogate treaty rights, however, the proposed revisions give undue power to applicants to disregard tribal sovereignty and treaty rights. LTBB requests that the ability to modify water quality certifications remains between the certifying authority and the federal agency. Neighboring Jurisdiction and Categorical Determinations The proposed revisions would significantly undermine tribal sovereignty by weakening neighboring jurisdiction analysis through the “may affect” process and categorical determinations of “no affect.” Unlike states, tribal lands constantly evolve through the acquisition of new parcels and new parcels entering “trust” status. Under the proposed rule, it would become very easy for tribal waters to be overlooked due to these shifting boundaries. Additionally, LTBB treaty rights extend beyond trust waters, throughout the entirety of the 1836 Ceded Territory. LTBB fears that categorical determinations of “no affect” would severely limit tribal notification and consultation, consequently severely impacting our treaty rights in waters across the 1836 Ceded Territory. As such, LTBB requests that the EPA develops a stronger analysis of neighboring jurisdictions and abandons any categorical determinations. Treatment as a State As noted in the proposed rule, the current rule creates an easier pathway for tribes with limited resources to protect water quality. CWA Section 303(c) TAS is difficult to obtain and implement for many tribes. By requiring tribes to secure Section 303(c) TAS prior to obtaining 401 TAS, the EPA is creating a significant barrier for tribes to protect our own waters and treaty-reserved rights. We would strongly urge the EPA to retain provisions from the 2023 Rule that allows tribes to receive Section 401 TAS without procuring Section 303(c) TAS. Conclusion The United States has a responsibility to protect LTBB’s treaty rights through adequate regulations that respect tribal sovereignty; however, the proposed revisions to Section 401 Water Quality Certification would significantly undermine tribal sovereignty and LTBB’s ability to protect our treaty-reserved rights to hunt, fish, and gather in the 1836 Ceded Territory as a whole. The proposed revisions are an affront to tribal sovereignty and the cooperative federalism that the Clean Water Act is built upon. LTBB requests that the EPA withdraw the proposed revisions to Section 401 regulations and instead conduct meaningful consultation with tribes to develop regulations that advance tribal sovereignty and the ability of tribes to protect our lands, waters, and treaty rights. In a good way, Caroline Moellering Environmental Services Program Manager Little Traverse Bay Bands of Odawa Indians
